Q1: I tried to set up a consistent study routine, aiming for about 20 hours a week spread across most days. I'm a visual learner, so I'd block out time to watch lecture videos and create diagrams or mind maps. Honestly though, even with the structure I tried to maintain, I found myself struggling to actually complete assignments despite putting in the study hours. I'd often spend time reviewing materials but then freeze up when it came to actually submitting work.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied more than I wanted it to. I'd start each week with good intentions and a schedule, but then deadlines or my extracurricular commitments would throw things off. When exams approached, everything became more chaotic and reactive. I tried to keep some consistency, but life kept getting in the way of my ideal routine.
Q3: Usually I'd log in when I got a notification about new content or when I remembered I needed to check something for an upcoming deadline. Sometimes I'd log in just to watch videos when I had downtime between other activities. Fear was honestly a big motivator too—I'd feel scared about falling behind, so I'd check in to reassure myself I knew what was happening in the course.
Q4: Yeah, for a while there logging in became pretty automatic, especially in the mornings before my other commitments. I'd grab coffee and scroll through the LMS to see what was new, watch some video content. It felt like checking social media almost—just part of my daily digital routine. The habit formed because I was genuinely trying to stay on top of things, even if my actual output didn't reflect that effort.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely essential for me—I watched over 100 minutes of content because that's how I learn best. Being able to pause, rewind, and visually see concepts explained made a huge difference. I also found the peer interactions helpful; connecting with other students through the platform gave me some sense of community and helped clarify things I was confused about.
Q6: I mostly ignored the discussion posts beyond a couple of contributions. I know participating more could have helped, but I felt scared of saying something wrong or looking stupid in front of everyone. Some of the reading materials I'd skim rather than deeply engage with because walls of text just don't stick for me the way visual content does. I kept telling myself I'd get to them later.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see immediate relevance to understanding the material or when it was visual and engaging. If I could watch something and feel like concepts were clicking into place, that was valuable. I also valued anything that helped me connect with peers because studying alone all the time was isolating and scary.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't afford mentally. With required work already overwhelming me, optional stuff got pushed aside even when it looked interesting. Required activities carried this weight of fear—like if I didn't do them, I'd fail. Optional ones didn't trigger that same urgency, so they fell off my radar even though they might have actually helped me more.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded a lot of materials and studied from them on my tablet without being connected to the LMS. I'd screenshot diagrams, save PDFs, and review them during commutes or when I was away from my computer. I also used YouTube videos and external resources to supplement what the course offered, especially when I needed different visual explanations of concepts.
Q10: There were many times I'd be studying intensely from printed notes or downloaded materials for hours, but the LMS would show nothing. I'd work through problems on paper, create visual study guides, or discuss concepts with friends in person. All that learning was real and meaningful, but it was completely invisible to the system tracking my engagement.
Q11: Definitely. I'd download lecture slides or videos once and then spend days working through them offline. My internet is reliable, but I preferred studying without the distraction of being online. I'd watch a video, download it, then rewatch sections multiple times on my own device. The LMS only captured that initial access, not all the subsequent learning.
Q12: Around midterms, everything got harder. I was scared about my exam performance because I could feel that despite all my studying, something wasn't translating to actual results. The fear made me freeze up on assignments—I'd spend hours preparing but then couldn't bring myself to submit work. My extracurriculars also ramped up, and balancing everything became overwhelming.
Q13: Fatigue hit me hard, especially trying to juggle 8 online courses simultaneously. There were days I'd log into the LMS but just stare at the screen, too mentally exhausted to actually process anything. Competing responsibilities from my extracurriculars meant I sometimes had to choose between engagement and other commitments. The confusion about why my effort wasn't translating to grades made me want to disengage.
Q14: Many times, honestly. I'd have the LMS open, videos playing, but my mind would be elsewhere—worrying about upcoming deadlines or feeling scared about my performance. I'd scroll through materials without really absorbing them. It was like going through the motions because I felt I should be studying, but the meaningful engagement just wasn't there.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even though I knew they were crucial. The fear of doing them wrong paralyzed me—I'd rather not submit than submit something bad. I'd tell myself I needed to understand the material better first, so I'd watch more videos instead of actually doing the work. It was a self-defeating cycle I couldn't break out of.
Q16: Deadlines created these intense spikes of activity for me. I'd suddenly log in multiple times a day as a deadline approached, frantically trying to prepare. But the fear often meant I'd engage with materials without actually completing submissions. The deadline pressure paradoxically made me more active on the LMS but less productive in terms of actual output.
Q17: When I did get feedback, it affected me a lot. Positive feedback gave me a temporary boost and made me want to engage more. But negative or critical feedback amplified my fear and sometimes made me avoid that area of the course entirely. I'd hesitate to check grades because I was scared of what I'd see, which delayed my response to feedback.
Q18: The course structure with 8 online courses running simultaneously was honestly too much for me to handle well. I appreciated having video content available since that matched my learning style, but the sheer volume of material across all courses meant I couldn't give this one the attention it deserved. The peer support intervention helped somewhat—having that connection made the course feel less isolating.
Q19: The video-heavy content made engagement easier because that's how I learn best. Having peer interactions available was also helpful. What made it harder was the assignment structure—I'd score well on assignments I completed but my completion rate was terrible. If there had been more scaffolding or smaller, lower-stakes submissions, maybe I wouldn't have frozen up so much.
Q20: I think that describes me pretty well, actually. I was highly active—logging in frequently, watching tons of videos, interacting with peers—but my actual submissions were minimal. For me, it was fear of failure that created that gap. I was engaged with learning but paralyzed when it came to producing work that would be evaluated. The activity was genuine; the output just didn't match.
Q21: I can see how that happens. Some people are just efficient—they download what they need, study offline, and perform well without constant LMS activity. I wish I was more like that. My high activity didn't translate to good performance, which suggests that raw engagement metrics don't capture whether someone is actually learning effectively or just spinning their wheels like I was.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of access, time spent on videos, and frequency of logins pretty accurately. But they completely miss the quality of that engagement—whether someone is actually learning or just anxiously clicking around. They miss offline studying, the emotional state behind the activity, and whether high engagement is productive or just fear-driven procrastination like mine often was.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely connecting with the material and feeling like you're making progress toward understanding it. It's not just logging in or watching videos—it's that feeling when concepts click and you can apply them. For me, true engagement also involves feeling part of a learning community, not isolated and scared. Unfortunately, I had the activity without always having that deeper connection.
Q24: The LMS data wouldn't show how scared I was throughout this course, or how that fear paralyzed my ability to submit work despite genuine effort to learn. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent studying offline, the peer conversations that happened outside the platform, or the mental energy I expended trying to overcome my anxiety about assignments. My engagement was real but my fear made it ineffective.
